title: Francophone-Anglophone gap in African interventional trial density
body: Does the language of colonisation predict access to clinical research in sub-Saharan Africa? We queried ClinicalTrials.gov for all interventional trials across ten Francophone and nine Anglophone African countries, computing per-capita trial density and condition-level counts for HIV, malaria, tuberculosis, cancer, and diabetes. Francophone Africa registered approximately 808 interventional trials compared with 6,690 in Anglophone Africa, yielding a per-capita density ratio of 5.5 to 1 after adjustment for population size. The gap persisted across all five disease categories and was most extreme in Chad, Togo, and Niger, which collectively registered fewer trials than a single US academic medical centre. Burkina Faso and Guinea partially escaped the desert through established malaria research hubs, demonstrating that targeted disease-specific infrastructure investment can overcome language barriers. These findings quantify a systematic exclusion of Francophone African populations from the evidence base informing treatment guidelines. The analysis is limited to a single English-language registry and may undercount trials on French-language or regional platforms.

sentences.text: Do Francophone and Anglophone African countries differ in registered interventional trial density after adjustment for population?

We queried ClinicalTrials.gov for all interventional trials across ten Francophone and nine Anglophone countries in sub-Saharan Africa and calculated trials per million population together with condition-level counts for HIV, malaria, tuberculosis, cancer, and diabetes.
Francophone Africa had about 808 registered interventional trials versus 6,690 in Anglophone Africa, corresponding to a 5.5-fold per-capita gap.
The deficit was present across all five disease groups and was most pronounced in the lowest-density countries, including Chad, Togo, and Niger.
Burkina Faso and Guinea were partial exceptions because malaria-focused research hubs raised their local counts.
The results indicate that trial activity is unevenly distributed across language groups within the same continent.
The comparison is limited to a single registry and may undercount studies captured in French-language or regional platforms.

outsideNote.data: ClinicalTrials.gov API v2, 10 Francophone + 9 Anglophone African countries, 5 conditions

ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used in early drafting. The final text was rewritten by the author, who takes full responsibility for the content.
